text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Menon  
Riverside Medical Centre  
14 Brook Street, London W1G 7HY  

Dear Dr Priya Menon,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 21-07-1988, Hospital No: M847520 NHS No. 6039182745  
Flat 3, 29 Willow Lane, London, SE11 5QR 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with history of head injury in adolescence/low mood under GP review/hypertension on treatment/uses smartwatch with fall-detection alerts/reports intermittent poor sleep/continues to live independently with community support. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Perindopril 4 mg once daily.  
Sertraline 50 mg once daily.  
Calcium + Vitamin D supplements.  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He attended alone but brought his smartwatch, which he uses for fall detection and activity tracking. He describes variable sleep and occasional missed breakfast doses when rushing to work; he has set phone reminders that help most days. He remains in full-time employment in IT support and commutes by bus. He denies recent head injuries and reports no current alcohol use; he drinks one to two coffees daily and does not smoke. Blood pressure in clinic was 128/76 mmHg, pulse 68 bpm regular. Neurological examination was non-focal. Gait narrow-based and steady; tandem gait mildly unsteady but safe. 

Seizures: Alex does not keep a written seizure journal and his recollection of exact dates is limited, so event frequency reporting is imperfect. He believes there were two brief episodes in the last six months characterised by a sudden blank spell with loss of awareness for under a minute, followed by fatigue. The smartwatch issued fall-detection notifications on 14 May and 22 August at 19:42 and 07:10 respectively; he recalls sitting down abruptly on the first occasion and waking on the sofa on the second. No injuries were reported. There were no emergency call activations logged on the device, and he did not attend A&E. No nocturnal tongue biting or urinary incontinence reported. He has not driven. 

Investigations: Last serum lamotrigine level not on record; routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC, bone profile, vitamin D) done by GP in March 2025 reportedly within reference ranges per patient. No MRI since 2019; EEG last performed 2020 (general report unavailable today—awaiting copy from records). 

Plan:  
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
- Request lamotrigine level, U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D via GP to coincide with next routine blood draw.  
- Provide Alex with a structured seizure log template; advise pairing manual entries with smartwatch alert timestamps to improve chronology.  
- With consent, enable sharing of smartwatch fall/impact and heart rate events to clinic email prior to next review.  
- Safety reinforced: avoid swimming unsupervised, take showers rather than baths, and maintain medication adherence using existing phone reminders.  
- No change to driving status as he has self-declared non-driving; to inform DVLA should events recur.  
- Review in 6 months, sooner if increase in events, injury, or medication intolerance. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He believes there were two brief episodes in the last six months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "He believes there were two brief episodes in the last six months." Clinic date is 02 October 2025; the smartwatch alerts on 14 May and 22 August fall within the last six months, supporting this count. The patient is therefore not seizure free for ≥6 months. Using the explicit numeric expression, the frequency is 2 per 6 months (equivalent to 1 per 3 months, but we prioritize the stated phrasing). No other higher-frequency seizure counts are provided.
seizure_frequency_number: He believes there were two brief episodes in the last six months

flaws.description: Gold (2 per 6 month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
